fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the first official entry into my Weeknotes! The infrastructure has long been in development, but I have been too lazy / scared to actually start making / publicizing these.

This week I spent the large majority of my time taking notes in this forest. We can see this via the 23hr spent on my forest throughout the week. Admittedly, some of this was spent purely on aesthetic re-design / cleanup, which I hope to not have to do this week. Lab corresponds to my kernel learning, where I spent what appears to be ~5hr implementing a few of the Triton tutorials, including GEMM. I played around with excalidraw and took some notes here. With this, I began looking into the Kernel DSLs landscape along with MLIR.

Outside of coding, I did not spend too much time brain-rotting. My phone screentime has also gotten remarkably low, so the issue is not really wasting time doomscrolling / watching youtube, etc. Instead, the problem is just a lack of maintained focus. I bounce between things too frequently, failing to get as much done as I should. I hope to remedy this by being disciplined with pomodoro blocks but early attempts have not been too promising.

I have been on a remarkable streak of bad luck when trying to run. During vacation (the 3 mile week), there was a heatwave going on throughout Europe. Then, upon my return, I got wildly sick (still managed to get to 30 miles but my Garmin did not like it). Then, this most recent week the Canadian wildfires made Michigan look like a scene out of Chernobyl. I tried to run, but my eyes began watering as soon as I got outside. Hopefully, I can get back on track and scale back up to 40mpw and then to 50+ before we get too close to the marathon.

In other news, I seem to be getting pretty consistent with my habits. I have remarkably caught up on my Anki deck and now need to do more exploratory study to have enough cards each day. The to-do list has not been as life-changing as I hoped as I still struggle to adhere to it on a daily basis. One reason is I think I am too ambitious and should instead start with just one must get done thing each day.

I have begun reading Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Goden Braid and am really enjoying it. It is strangely refreshing having the math I learned so rigorously distilled into much more digestible chunks. I have also gotten halfway through Candide, which is a very different read (thoughts coming soon).

This was a very different read for me. A short book (only 135 pages) that goes into great detail the work day at the Mezzanine. I have some gripes with the description of readable on the cover. I found the incredibly vivid imagery sometimes hard to follow. It seems the intent of the book was to highlight the thoughts of an ordinary working man throughout the day. However, some of these thoughs were so far from ordinary it lost the relatable, funny read it was going for. Some chapters were pure gold (the urinal) but others were a huge miss for me.
Overall, I enjoyed the book and would recommend. 6.5/10.

html:figure.html:figcaption: One of many book covers. Not the best IMO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I completed a rough outline of the state of kernel generations as far as I am concerned. I hope to make some serious progress on this next week. Namely, I want to have a pretty complete understanding of the Tiled DSLs portion and where the distinction between each DSL is made. I would like to deeply understand the Flash attention Triton kernel from the tutorial. Afterwards, moving to TileLang and seeing how / where they draw the line for more optimization opportunity.
For Reading Corner, I will likely finish Candide and might also finish Elon Musk, which I have been working on for awhile now. Stay tuned for their reviews!

fr:title.text: TileLang: A Composable Tiled Programming Model for AI Systems

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: TileLang: A Composable Tiled Programming Model for AI Systems
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @misc{wangTileLangComposableTiled2025, title = {{{TileLang}}: {{A Composable Tiled Programming Model}} for {{AI Systems}}}, author = {Wang, Lei and Cheng, Yu and Shi, Yining and Tang, Zhengju and Mo, Zhiwen and Xie, Wenhao and Ma, Lingxiao and Xia, Yuqing and Xue, Jilong and Yang, Fan and Yang, Zhi}, year = {2025}, doi = {10.48550/arXiv.2504.17577}, urldate = {2026-07-13}, number = {arXiv:2504.17577}, publisher = {arXiv}, file = {/home/kellen/Downloads/papers/TileLang A Composable Tiled Programming Model for AI Systems - Wang et al..pdf}, keywords = {Computer Science - Machine Learning}, langid = {english}, archiveprefix = {arXiv}, abstract = {Modern AI workloads rely heavily on optimized computing kernels for both training and inference. These AI kernels follow well-defined data-flow patterns, such as moving tiles between DRAM and SRAM and performing a sequence of computations on those tiles. However, writing high-performance kernels remains complex despite the clarity of these patterns. Achieving peak performance requires careful, hardware-centric optimizations to fully leverage modern accelerators. While domain-specific compilers attempt to reduce the burden of writing high-performance kernels, they often struggle with usability and expressiveness gaps. In this paper, we present TileLang, a generalized tiled programming model for more efficient AI Kernel programming. TileLang decouples scheduling space (thread binding, layout, tensorize and pipeline) from dataflow, and encapsulated them as a set of customization annotations and primitives. This approach allows users to focus on the kernel's data-flow itself, while leaving most other optimizations to compilers. We conduct comprehensive experiments on commonly-used devices, across numerous experiments, our evaluation shows that TileLang can achieve state-of-the-art performance in key kernels, demonstrating that its unified block-and-thread paradigm and transparent scheduling capabilities deliver both the power and flexibility demanded by modern AI system development.}, primaryclass = {cs.LG}, eprint = {2504.17577}, month = {April}, shorttitle = {{{TileLang}}} }

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Elon Musk
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @book{isaacsonElonMusk2023, title = {Elon {{Musk}}}, author = {Isaacson, Walter}, year = {2023}, isbn = {978-1-76142-262-1}, publisher = {{Simon and Schuster}}, keywords = {Biography & Autobiography / Business,Biography & Autobiography / General,Biography & Autobiography / Rich & Famous,Biography & Autobiography / Science & Technology}, langid = {english}, googlebooks = {6\_mzEAAAQBAJ}, abstract = {From the author of Steve Jobs and other bestselling biographies, this is the astonishingly intimate story of the most fascinating and controversial innovator of our era---a rule-breaking visionary who helped to lead the world into the era of electric vehicles, private space exploration, and artificial intelligence. Oh, and took over Twitter.Australian Financial Review Top 20 Read for 2023\hspace{0pt} When Elon Musk was a kid in South Africa, he was regularly beaten by bullies. One day a group pushed him down some concrete steps and kicked him until his face was a swollen ball of flesh. He was in the hospital for a week. But the physical scars were minor compared to the emotional ones inflicted by his father, an engineer, rogue, and charismatic fantasist. His father's impact on his psyche would linger. He developed into a tough yet vulnerable man-child, prone to abrupt Jekyll-and-Hyde mood swings, with an exceedingly high tolerance for risk, a craving for drama, an epic sense of mission, and a maniacal intensity that was callous and at times destructive. At the beginning of 2022---after a year marked by SpaceX launching thirty-one rockets into orbit, Tesla selling a million cars, and him becoming the richest man on earth---Musk spoke ruefully about his compulsion to stir up dramas. ``I need to shift my mindset away from being in crisis mode, which it has been for about fourteen years now, or arguably most of my life,'' he said. It was a wistful comment, not a New Year's resolution. Even as he said it, he was secretly buying up shares of Twitter, the world's ultimate playground. Over the years, whenever he was in a dark place, his mind went back to being bullied on the playground. Now he had the chance to own the playground. For two years, Isaacson shadowed Musk, attended his meetings, walked his factories with him, and spent hours interviewing him, his family, friends, coworkers, and adversaries. The result is the revealing inside story, filled with amazing tales of triumphs and turmoil, that addresses the question: are the demons that drive Musk also what it takes to drive innovation and progress?}, month = {September} }

fr:title.text: Triton: An intermediate language and compiler for tiled neural network computations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Triton: An intermediate language and compiler for tiled neural network computations
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @inproceedings{tilletTritonIntermediateLanguage2019, title = {Triton: An Intermediate Language and Compiler for Tiled Neural Network Computations}, author = {Tillet, Philippe and Kung, H. T. and Cox, David}, year = {2019}, isbn = {978-1-4503-6719-6}, doi = {10.1145/3315508.3329973}, urldate = {2026-06-23}, booktitle = {Proceedings of the 3rd {{ACM SIGPLAN International Workshop}} on {{Machine Learning}} and {{Programming Languages}}}, pages = {10--19}, publisher = {ACM}, address = {Phoenix AZ USA}, file = {/home/kellen/Downloads/papers/Triton an intermediate language and compiler for tiled neural network computations - Tillet et al..pdf}, langid = {english}, abstract = {The validation and deployment of novel research ideas in the field of Deep Learning is often limited by the availability of efficient compute kernels for certain basic primitives. In particular, operations that cannot leverage existing vendor libraries (e.g., cuBLAS, cuDNN) are at risk of facing poor device utilization unless custom implementations are written by experts -- usually at the expense of portability. For this reason, the development of new programming abstractions for specifying custom Deep Learning workloads at a minimal performance cost has become crucial.}, month = {June}, shorttitle = {Triton} }

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A tile-based Kernel DSLs lowered to backend agnostic MLIR.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Candide
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @book{voltaireCandide2015, title = {Candide}, author = {Voltaire}, year = {2015}, isbn = {978-1-4677-7800-8}, publisher = {First Avenue Editions}, keywords = {Fiction / Classics,Fiction / Political,Fiction / Satire}, langid = {english}, googlebooks = {Iy5TBgAAQBAJ}, abstract = {Though he\&\#39;s the illegitimate nephew of a German baron, Candide grows up in a castle under the tutelage of the scholar Pangloss. Pangloss is so enraptured by the Enlightenment\~Nan era of prosperity and intellectual growth\~Nthat he proclaims the world to be \"the best of all possible worlds.\" As an adult, Candide tries to cling to this optimistic philosophy despite experiencing a series of horrible misfortunes while striving to be reunited with the woman he loves. The French novel Candide satirizes the philosophies of the Enlightenment and humorously criticizes the nobility, religious viewpoints, and politics of the time. Voltaire\~Nthe pen name of French author Fran?ois-Marie Arouet\~Nfirst published his satire in 1759. This is an unabridged version of an English translation, published in 1918.}, month = {January} }

fr:title.text: Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Goden Braid

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Goden Braid
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @book{hofstadterGodelEscherBach1999, title = {G\"odel, {{Escher}}, {{Bach}}: {{An Eternal Goden Braid}}}, author = {Hofstadter, Douglas R.}, year = {1999}, isbn = {978-0-465-02656-2}, publisher = {Basic Books}, keywords = {Art / Individual Artists / General,Biography & Autobiography / Science & Technology,Computers / Intelligence (AI) & Semantics,Mathematics / Logic,Music / Genres & Styles / Classical,Philosophy / General,Science / General,Science / Philosophy & Social Aspects}, langid = {english}, googlebooks = {uZLdksxOEecC}, abstract = {Douglas Hofstadter's book is concerned directly with the nature of ?maps'' or links between formal systems. However, according to Hofstadter, the formal system that underlies all mental activity transcends the system that supports it. If life can grow out of the formal chemical substrate of the cell, if consciousness can emerge out of a formal system of firing neurons, then so too will computers attain human intelligence. G\"odel Escher and Bach is a wonderful exploration of fascinating ideas at the heart of cognitive science: meaning, reduction, recursion, and much more.}, shorttitle = {G\"odel, {{Escher}}, {{Bach}}} }

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The Mezzanine

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Here I will document my progress for the week along with other miscellaneous things I feel like disclosing publicly.
This is the first official entry into my Weeknotes! The infrastructure has long been in development, but I have been too lazy / scared to actually start making / publicizing these.

This week I spent the large majority of my time taking notes in this forest. We can see this via the 23hr spent on my forest throughout the week. Admittedly, some of this was spent purely on aesthetic re-design / cleanup, which I hope to not have to do this week. Lab corresponds to my kernel learning, where I spent what appears to be ~5hr implementing a few of the Triton tutorials, including GEMM. I played around with excalidraw and took some notes here. With this, I began looking into the Kernel DSLs landscape along with MLIR.

Outside of coding, I did not spend too much time brain-rotting. My phone screentime has also gotten remarkably low, so the issue is not really wasting time doomscrolling / watching youtube, etc. Instead, the problem is just a lack of maintained focus. I bounce between things too frequently, failing to get as much done as I should. I hope to remedy this by being disciplined with pomodoro blocks but early attempts have not been too promising.

I have been on a remarkable streak of bad luck when trying to run. During vacation (the 3 mile week), there was a heatwave going on throughout Europe. Then, upon my return, I got wildly sick (still managed to get to 30 miles but my Garmin did not like it). Then, this most recent week the Canadian wildfires made Michigan look like a scene out of Chernobyl. I tried to run, but my eyes began watering as soon as I got outside. Hopefully, I can get back on track and scale back up to 40mpw and then to 50+ before we get too close to the marathon.

In other news, I seem to be getting pretty consistent with my habits. I have remarkably caught up on my Anki deck and now need to do more exploratory study to have enough cards each day. The to-do list has not been as life-changing as I hoped as I still struggle to adhere to it on a daily basis. One reason is I think I am too ambitious and should instead start with just one must get done thing each day.

I have begun reading Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Goden Braid and am really enjoying it. It is strangely refreshing having the math I learned so rigorously distilled into much more digestible chunks. I have also gotten halfway through Candide, which is a very different read (thoughts coming soon).

This was a very different read for me. A short book (only 135 pages) that goes into great detail the work day at the Mezzanine. I have some gripes with the description of readable on the cover. I found the incredibly vivid imagery sometimes hard to follow. It seems the intent of the book was to highlight the thoughts of an ordinary working man throughout the day. However, some of these thoughs were so far from ordinary it lost the relatable, funny read it was going for. Some chapters were pure gold (the urinal) but others were a huge miss for me.
Overall, I enjoyed the book and would recommend. 6.5/10.

html:figure.html:figcaption: One of many book covers. Not the best IMO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I completed a rough outline of the state of kernel generations as far as I am concerned. I hope to make some serious progress on this next week. Namely, I want to have a pretty complete understanding of the Tiled DSLs portion and where the distinction between each DSL is made. I would like to deeply understand the Flash attention Triton kernel from the tutorial. Afterwards, moving to TileLang and seeing how / where they draw the line for more optimization opportunity.
For Reading Corner, I will likely finish Candide and might also finish Elon Musk, which I have been working on for awhile now. Stay tuned for their reviews!

fr:link.title: Halide: A Language and Compiler for Optimizing Parallelism, Locality, and Recomputation in Image Processing Pipelines

html:li.fr:link: Halide: A Language and Compiler for Optimizing Parallelism, Locality, and Recomputation in Image Processing Pipelines

fr:link.title: TVM: An Automated End-to-End Optimizing Compiler for Deep Learning

html:li.fr:link: TVM: An Automated End-to-End Optimizing Compiler for Deep Learning

fr:link.title: PyTorch 2: Faster Machine Learning Through Dynamic Python Bytecode Transformation and Graph Compilation

html:li.fr:link: PyTorch 2: Faster Machine Learning Through Dynamic Python Bytecode Transformation and Graph Compilation

fr:link.title: Triton: An intermediate language and compiler for tiled neural network computations

html:li.fr:link: Triton: An intermediate language and compiler for tiled neural network computations

fr:link.title: TileLang: A Composable Tiled Programming Model for AI Systems

html:li.fr:link: TileLang: A Composable Tiled Programming Model for AI Systems

fr:link.title: Helion: A High-Level DSL for Performant and Portable ML Kernels

html:li.fr:link: Helion: A High-Level DSL for Performant and Portable ML Kernels

fr:link.title: TIRx: An Open Compiler Stack for Evolving Frontier ML Kernels

html:li.fr:link: TIRx: An Open Compiler Stack for Evolving Frontier ML Kernels

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Backlink accumulator for all things kernel DSLs.

This was a very different read for me. A short book (only 135 pages) that goes into great detail the work day at the Mezzanine. I have some gripes with the description of readable on the cover. I found the incredibly vivid imagery sometimes hard to follow. It seems the intent of the book was to highlight the thoughts of an ordinary working man throughout the day. However, some of these thoughs were so far from ordinary it lost the relatable, funny read it was going for. Some chapters were pure gold (the urinal) but others were a huge miss for me.
Overall, I enjoyed the book and would recommend. 6.5/10.

html:figure.html:figcaption: One of many book covers. Not the best IMO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Overall, I thoroughly enjoyed this book. As a reinforcement learning afficianado, I have a newfound fandom for the mission of Deepmind. In particular, I was at the RLC talk given by David Silver mentioned in the book. However, I must acknowledge the deep bias in favor of Demis and Deepmind in the LLM race towards the end. It is easy to come away from this, thinking that Sam Altman is some sort of Silicon Valley super villain and Demis is our champion of humanity.
If I had to give an unbiased rating, I would give this book a 6.5/10. However, as a biased reader, I give it an 8/10 :)

This was basically a historical account of the history of deep learning. The main focus was championing the likes of Hinton, Lecun, and Bengio with their unwavering conviction toward deep learning. Honestly, this book has made me excited and a bit intimidated about research. I do not know if I have the same obsessive commitment to my work as Hinton, etc. Also, for future reference, I feel like I will get a lot more out of books I am not already so familiar with. Technically, I thought it did an OK job, but I once again do not really enjoy reading books on my field for a broad audience. Learning about the industry origins i.e. DNN, Deepmind acquisition, etc. was pretty interesting though.
Enjoyable read. 5/10.
Here I will document my progress for the week along with other miscellaneous things I feel like disclosing publicly.
This is the first official entry into my Weeknotes! The infrastructure has long been in development, but I have been too lazy / scared to actually start making / publicizing these.

This week I spent the large majority of my time taking notes in this forest. We can see this via the 23hr spent on my forest throughout the week. Admittedly, some of this was spent purely on aesthetic re-design / cleanup, which I hope to not have to do this week. Lab corresponds to my kernel learning, where I spent what appears to be ~5hr implementing a few of the Triton tutorials, including GEMM. I played around with excalidraw and took some notes here. With this, I began looking into the Kernel DSLs landscape along with MLIR.

Outside of coding, I did not spend too much time brain-rotting. My phone screentime has also gotten remarkably low, so the issue is not really wasting time doomscrolling / watching youtube, etc. Instead, the problem is just a lack of maintained focus. I bounce between things too frequently, failing to get as much done as I should. I hope to remedy this by being disciplined with pomodoro blocks but early attempts have not been too promising.

I have been on a remarkable streak of bad luck when trying to run. During vacation (the 3 mile week), there was a heatwave going on throughout Europe. Then, upon my return, I got wildly sick (still managed to get to 30 miles but my Garmin did not like it). Then, this most recent week the Canadian wildfires made Michigan look like a scene out of Chernobyl. I tried to run, but my eyes began watering as soon as I got outside. Hopefully, I can get back on track and scale back up to 40mpw and then to 50+ before we get too close to the marathon.

In other news, I seem to be getting pretty consistent with my habits. I have remarkably caught up on my Anki deck and now need to do more exploratory study to have enough cards each day. The to-do list has not been as life-changing as I hoped as I still struggle to adhere to it on a daily basis. One reason is I think I am too ambitious and should instead start with just one must get done thing each day.

I have begun reading Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Goden Braid and am really enjoying it. It is strangely refreshing having the math I learned so rigorously distilled into much more digestible chunks. I have also gotten halfway through Candide, which is a very different read (thoughts coming soon).

This was a very different read for me. A short book (only 135 pages) that goes into great detail the work day at the Mezzanine. I have some gripes with the description of readable on the cover. I found the incredibly vivid imagery sometimes hard to follow. It seems the intent of the book was to highlight the thoughts of an ordinary working man throughout the day. However, some of these thoughs were so far from ordinary it lost the relatable, funny read it was going for. Some chapters were pure gold (the urinal) but others were a huge miss for me.
Overall, I enjoyed the book and would recommend. 6.5/10.

html:figure.html:figcaption: One of many book covers. Not the best IMO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I completed a rough outline of the state of kernel generations as far as I am concerned. I hope to make some serious progress on this next week. Namely, I want to have a pretty complete understanding of the Tiled DSLs portion and where the distinction between each DSL is made. I would like to deeply understand the Flash attention Triton kernel from the tutorial. Afterwards, moving to TileLang and seeing how / where they draw the line for more optimization opportunity.
For Reading Corner, I will likely finish Candide and might also finish Elon Musk, which I have been working on for awhile now. Stay tuned for their reviews!
